verbatim_text: And really this type of inference by optimization is very classical in AI or probabilistic inference or this stuff, right? There is a lot of really classic, you know, a lot of classic work in past planning. You know, all kinds of planning actually, you know, shortest path between cities, circuit between cities, sat, you know, finding values of bullion, variables that satisfy bullion formula, logical inference, all of those things can be reduced to optimization problems, but not necessarily to forward propagation to a fixed number of layers. So this, this inference by optimization allows for what some people call zero shot learning, which means producing answers or solution to problems without being trained to produce solutions to that problem. Basically just coming up with a new solution to a problem. This is what search and optimization can do. This is also perhaps a good model for the type of inference that takes place in humans, that psychological system two. So system one is, you know, decisions you're making or actions you're taking instinctively, basically without really having to think about it too much. And system two is the, the type of actions that you, or decisions you make deliberately by thinking about it and maybe using your mental model of the world to predict the outcome of particular actions you might take. Now, this is not what LMS are doing. Lms take a a window of a sequence over a sequence of symbols, then run, run that through some big neural net and produce. The next a guess as to what the next symbol is. And then once you have produced the next symbol, you shift it into the input and. Then you produce a second symbol, shift that into the input, third symbol, et cetera.
